---
title: "Project 3 - Nikola"
author:
  - name: "Nikola Meshkovski"

institute: Corvinus University of Budapest
date: 2026-02-26

format:
  pdf:
    latex-engine: xelatex

fontsize: 12pt
mainfont: "Times New Roman"
number-sections: true

execute:
  eval: true          # Evaluate code chunks
  include: true       # Include both code and results in output
  echo: true          # Show source code in output
  output: true        # Show results of code execution
  warning: false      # Suppress warnings
  message: false      # Suppress messages
  cache: true         # Chunk caching (set to true for faster rebuilds)

knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    fig.path: "figs/" # Folder where all figures will be saved
---

```{r}
#| label: Clean Enviroment

# Clean Environment
rm(list=ls())

```

```{r}
#| label: Packages

# Load packages
packages <- c(
  "readxl",      # Read Excel files (.xls, .xlsx)
  "tidyverse",   # Data manipulation and visualisation (ggplot2, dplyr, etc.)
  "cluster",     # Clustering algorithms (k-means, hierarchical, etc.)
  "factoextra",  # Visualise clustering and PCA results
  "here",        # Builds paths relative to the project root (.qmd location)
  "renv",
  "plm",
  "tseries",
  "writexl",
  "sf",
  "rnaturalearth",
  "rnaturalearthdata",
  "countrycode",
  "fixest"
)

for (pkg in packages) {
  if (!require(pkg, character.only = TRUE)) {
    install.packages(pkg)
    library(pkg, character.only = TRUE)
  }
}

## Done already
# renv::init() is only run once when setting up the project for the first time. 
# After that, renv::snapshot() is what we use to record any new packages into renv.lock.

# Set global ggplot2 theme: Times New Roman font for all figures
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12, base_family = "serif"))
theme_update(text = element_text(family = "serif"))

```

```{r}
#| label: Set Working Directory & Data Loading

# Load data — paths relative to materials/
df_main <- read_excel(here("data", "data_raw_theglobaleconomy.xlsx"))
df_monetary_policy <- read_excel(here("data", "data_raw_monetary-transmission.xlsx"), 
                                  sheet = "data_CWY_annual")

# Load real GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$) from World Bank
# skip=3 bypasses the 3 metadata rows; row 4 in the file is the true header
df_gdp_real <- read_excel(here("data", "Real_GDP_pc_WorldBank.xls"), skip = 3) |>
  select(`Country Name`, `1960`:`2025`) |>             # keep country + all year cols
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = -`Country Name`,
    names_to  = "Year",
    values_to = "GDP_pc_real"
  ) |>
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year)) |>
  rename(Country = `Country Name`)

```


```{r}
#| label: Merging Datasets

# Keep only MPS from the monetary policy dataset
mps_subset <- df_monetary_policy[, c("Country", "Year", "MPS")]

# Merge MPS into the main dataset
df_world <- merge(
  df_main,
  mps_subset,
  by     = c("Country", "Year"),
  all.x  = TRUE
)

# Drop the nominal GDP per capita column and replace with real GDP per capita
df_world <- df_world |>
  select(-`GDP per capita, current U.S. dollars`) |>   # ← remove nominal variable
  merge(
    df_gdp_real,
    by     = c("Country", "Year"),
    all.x  = TRUE
  )

# Sort by Country and Year for readability
df_world <- df_world[order(df_world$Country, df_world$Year), ]

# Clean up
rm(df_main, df_monetary_policy, mps_subset, df_gdp_real)

```


```{r}
#| label: Choosing initial time intervals

# Most likely time range for availiable data: 2000–2019 (Annual)
# Isolates the COVID pandemic which would be an outlier, and Monetary Policy Transmission only has data until 2019
df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world %>%
  filter(Year >= 2000, Year <= 2019)

# Also filter df_world to be after 2000, data from 1990 is too broad
df_world <- df_world %>%
  filter(Year >= 2000, Year <= 2026)

```


```{r}
#| label: Initial missingness table


# Create a table with missing observations for each variable
missing_table <- data.frame(
  Missing_Obs = sapply(df_world_2000_2019, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Total_Obs = nrow(df_world_2000_2019)
)

# Calculate the percentage of missingness for each variable
missing_table$Missing_Percent <- round(
  (missing_table$Missing_Obs / missing_table$Total_Obs) * 100, 2
)

# Sort the table from highest to lowest missingness percentage
missing_table <- missing_table[order(-missing_table$Missing_Percent), ]

# View the result
missing_table

```

```{r}
#| label: Development status segmentation by standard World Bank/IMF threshold


# Create a categorical variable based on GDP per capita ()
# Countries with GDP per capita above 14,000 are labeled "Developed"
# Countries with GDP per capita below 14,000 are labeled "EMDEs (Emerging market and developing economies)"
# Missing GDP per capita values stay as NA

# Compute each country's average GDP per capita over the full 2000-2019 window
avg_gdp <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  group_by(Country) %>%
  summarise(avg_gdp_pc = mean(`GDP_pc_real`, na.rm = TRUE))

# Apply the $14,000 threshold once — produces a single fixed label per country
avg_gdp$DevelopmentStatus <- ifelse(
  is.na(avg_gdp$avg_gdp_pc), NA,
  ifelse(avg_gdp$avg_gdp_pc > 14000, "Developed", "Emerging")
)

# Binary version: 1 = Developed, 0 = Emerging
avg_gdp$DevelopmentStatus_binary <- ifelse(
  is.na(avg_gdp$avg_gdp_pc), NA,
  ifelse(avg_gdp$avg_gdp_pc > 14000, 1, 0)
)

# Merge the fixed classification back — every row for a country gets the same label
df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  left_join(avg_gdp[, c("Country", "DevelopmentStatus", "DevelopmentStatus_binary")],
            by = "Country")

# Clean up helper table
rm(avg_gdp)

```

```{r}
#| label: Missingness table by development status part 1


# Split the dataset into Emerging and Developed groups
df_emerging <- subset(df_world_2000_2019, DevelopmentStatus == "Emerging")
df_developed <- subset(df_world_2000_2019, DevelopmentStatus == "Developed")

# Create a missingness table for the Emerging group
missing_emerging <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(df_emerging),
  Missing_Obs = sapply(df_emerging, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Total_Obs = nrow(df_emerging)
)

# Calculate the percentage of missingness for the Emerging group
missing_emerging$Missing_Percent <- round(
  (missing_emerging$Missing_Obs / missing_emerging$Total_Obs) * 100, 2
)

# Sort the Emerging table by missingness percentage
missing_emerging <- missing_emerging[order(-missing_emerging$Missing_Percent), ]

# Create a missingness table for the Developed group
missing_developed <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(df_developed),
  Missing_Obs = sapply(df_developed, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Total_Obs = nrow(df_developed)
)

# Calculate the percentage of missingness for the Developed group
missing_developed$Missing_Percent <- round(
  (missing_developed$Missing_Obs / missing_developed$Total_Obs) * 100, 2
)

# Sort the Developed table by missingness percentage
missing_developed <- missing_developed[order(-missing_developed$Missing_Percent), ]

# View both tables (missingness by segment)
missing_emerging
missing_developed

# Export tables using relative paths anchored to materials/
write_xlsx(missing_emerging, here("tables", "descriptive_tables", "missing_emerging.xlsx"))
write_xlsx(missing_developed, here("tables", "descriptive_tables", "missing_developed.xlsx"))



```


```{r}
#| label: Missingness table by development status part 2


# Create missingness summary for the Emerging group
missing_emerging <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(df_emerging),
  Emerging_Missing_Obs = sapply(df_emerging, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Emerging_Total_Obs = nrow(df_emerging)
)

# Calculate percentage missingness for the Emerging group
missing_emerging$Emerging_Missing_Percent <- round(
  (missing_emerging$Emerging_Missing_Obs / missing_emerging$Emerging_Total_Obs) * 100, 2
)

# Create missingness summary for the Developed group
missing_developed <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(df_developed),
  Developed_Missing_Obs = sapply(df_developed, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Developed_Total_Obs = nrow(df_developed)
)

# Calculate percentage missingness for the Developed group
missing_developed$Developed_Missing_Percent <- round(
  (missing_developed$Developed_Missing_Obs / missing_developed$Developed_Total_Obs) * 100, 2
)

# Keep only the key comparison columns from each table
missing_emerging <- missing_emerging[, c("Variable", "Emerging_Missing_Obs", "Emerging_Missing_Percent")]
missing_developed <- missing_developed[, c("Variable", "Developed_Missing_Obs", "Developed_Missing_Percent")]

# Merge the two tables by variable name
missing_comparison <- merge(missing_emerging, missing_developed, by = "Variable", all = TRUE)

# Sort the combined table by Emerging missingness percentage, highest first
missing_comparison <- missing_comparison[order(-missing_comparison$Emerging_Missing_Percent), ]

# View the combined comparison table
missing_comparison

```


```{r}
#| label: Descriptive statistics by development status


# See data types within data frame
str(df_world_2000_2019)

# Keep only numeric variables for descriptive statistics
# This avoids trying to summarize text variables such as Country or DevelopmentStatus
numeric_emerging <- df_emerging[, sapply(df_emerging, is.numeric)]
numeric_developed <- df_developed[, sapply(df_developed, is.numeric)]

# Create descriptive statistics table for Emerging countries
# We calculate the number of non-missing observations, mean, standard deviation,
# minimum, median, and maximum for each numeric variable
desc_emerging <- data.frame(
  N = sapply(numeric_emerging, function(x) sum(!is.na(x))),
  Mean = sapply(numeric_emerging, function(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  SD = sapply(numeric_emerging, function(x) sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Min = sapply(numeric_emerging, function(x) min(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Median = sapply(numeric_emerging, function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Max = sapply(numeric_emerging, function(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE))
)

# Round the numeric results for a cleaner table
desc_emerging[, -c(1, 2)] <- round(desc_emerging[, -c(1, 2)], 2)

# Create descriptive statistics table for Developed countries
desc_developed <- data.frame(
  N = sapply(numeric_developed, function(x) sum(!is.na(x))),
  Mean = sapply(numeric_developed, function(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  SD = sapply(numeric_developed, function(x) sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Min = sapply(numeric_developed, function(x) min(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Median = sapply(numeric_developed, function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Max = sapply(numeric_developed, function(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE))
)

# Round the numeric results for a cleaner table
desc_developed[, -c(1, 2)] <- round(desc_developed[, -c(1, 2)], 2)

# View the two tables
desc_emerging
desc_developed

# Export tables using relative paths anchored to materials/
write_xlsx(desc_emerging, here("tables", "descriptive_tables", "desc_emerging.xlsx"))
write_xlsx(desc_developed, here("tables", "descriptive_tables", "desc_developed.xlsx"))

```


```{r}
#| label: Variable Selection

# --- DROP: Informal employment (~87% missing — unusable) ---
# --- DROP: Shadow economy (keep MIMIC version only — see below) ---
# --- NOTE: Bank lending-deposit spread (~44% missing) and real interest rate (~35% missing)
#           are excluded from the main specification due to high missingness, but are not dropped (might be used later).
#           This will be discussed in the data section of the paper. ---


# Keep only the variables needed for the analysis
df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(
    -'Shadow economy, percent of GDP',
    -'Informal employment, percent of total employment'
    )

```

```{r}
#| label: Clean Up Environment

rm(
  desc_developed,
  desc_emerging, 
  missing_comparison, 
  missing_developed, 
  missing_emerging, 
  missing_table, 
  numeric_developed, 
  numeric_emerging,
  packages,
  pkg,
  df_world,
  df_developed,
  df_emerging
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: Renaming Variables

# Rename all variables to short, code-friendly names
df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  rename(
    # --- Identifiers ---
    country = Country,
    year = Year,
    code = Code,
    continent_code = ContinentCode,

    # --- Classification ---
    dev_status = DevelopmentStatus,
    dev_binary = DevelopmentStatus_binary,

    # --- Key variable ---
    mps = MPS,                                                                  # Monetary Policy Sensitivity

    # --- Shadow economy ---
    mimic = `Informal economy, MIMIC method`,                                   # Shadow economy — MIMIC method

    # --- Macroeconomic variables ---
    gdp_pc = `GDP_pc_real`,                                                     # Real GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)
    gdp_growth = `Economic growth: the rate of change of real GDP`,             # Real GDP growth rate (annual %)
    infl = `Inflation: percent change in the Consumer Price Index`,             # CPI inflation (annual %)
    credit_gdp = `Domestic credit to the private sector, percent of GDP`,       # Domestic credit to private sector (% of GDP)
    hh_consumption = `Household consumption as percent of GDP`,                 # Household consumption (% of GDP)
    tax_rate = `Tax rate, percent of commercial profits`,                       # Tax rate (% of commercial profits)

    # --- Governance ---
    reg_quality = `Regulatory quality index (-2.5 weak; 2.5 strong)`,           # Regulatory quality (WGI)
    corruption = `Control of corruption (-2.5 weak; 2.5 strong)`,               # Control of corruption (WGI)
    pol_stability = `Political stability index (-2.5 weak; 2.5 strong)`,        # Political stability (WGI)
    voice_acc = `Voice and accountability index (-2.5 weak; 2.5 strong)`,       # Voice and accountability (WGI)
    pol_rights = `Political rights index, 7 (weak) - 1 (strong)`,               # Political rights index (Freedom House)

    # --- Excluded from main spec — high missingness, retained for robustness ---
    spread = `Bank lending-deposit interest rate spread`,                       # Lending-deposit spread (~44% missing)
    real_int_rate = `Real interest rate: Bank lending rate minus inflation`     # Real interest rate (~35% missing)
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: Diagnostics 1.0 Panel Unit Root Tests
#| output: true
#| warning: false


# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DIAGNOSTIC 1 — PANEL UNIT ROOT TESTS (Country-by-Country ADF approach)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# We need to know whether each variable is stationary (i.e., mean-reverting)
# or has a unit root (i.e., trends/wanders without returning to a stable mean).
# This matters because:
#   - If a variable is stationary → safe to use in levels in the LP regression.
#   - If a variable has a unit root → we should use it in first differences
#     or cumulative changes, otherwise regressions may be spurious.

# WHY NOT plm::purtest (IPS)?
# The Im-Pesaran-Shin test implemented in plm::purtest is fragile with
# unbalanced panels. With T=20 and gaps in coverage, AIC lag selection can
# consume too many degrees of freedom, producing NaN. Rather than fighting
# the package, we run individual ADF tests per country using tseries::adf.test
# and summarise across countries — this is essentially what IPS does under
# the hood (averaging country-level ADF statistics).

# THE ADF TEST:
#   H0: The series has a unit root (non-stationary)
#   H1: The series is stationary
#   Low p-value (< 0.05) → reject H0 → evidence of stationarity

# HOW TO INTERPRET THE SUMMARY TABLE:
#   - n_countries: how many countries had enough data to run the test
#   - mean_pval / median_pval: average p-values across countries
#     (lower = stronger overall evidence of stationarity)
#   - pct_rejected_5: percentage of countries where H0 was rejected at 5%
#     * > 70% rejected → variable is broadly stationary across the panel
#     * 30–70% rejected → mixed evidence, proceed with caution
#     * < 30% rejected → likely non-stationary, consider differencing

# NOTE: We require at least 15 non-missing observations per country.
# The ADF test needs enough data points to estimate an autoregression
# with lags — with T=20, requiring 15 usable obs is a reasonable floor.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════


# Variables to test: all key variables that will enter the LP regressions
# MPS is excluded — it is a shock series centered on zero by construction,
# so it is stationary by design and does not need testing.
vars_urt <- c("infl", "gdp_pc", "gdp_growth", "mimic", "credit_gdp",
              "reg_quality", "corruption", "pol_stability")

min_obs <- 15  # Minimum usable observations per country

results <- list()

for (v in vars_urt) {

  # Step 1: For each country, check if there are enough non-missing observations
  #         to run an ADF test. If yes, run adf.test and extract the p-value.
  #         If the test fails (e.g., constant series), return NA.
  country_pvals <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    group_by(country) %>%
    filter(sum(!is.na(.data[[v]])) >= min_obs) %>%
    summarise(
      adf_pval = tryCatch({
        adf.test(na.omit(.data[[v]]))$p.value
      }, error = function(e) NA_real_),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(adf_pval))

  # Step 2: Summarise across all countries that were successfully tested
  results[[v]] <- data.frame(
    variable       = v,
    n_countries    = nrow(country_pvals),
    mean_pval      = round(mean(country_pvals$adf_pval), 3),
    median_pval    = round(median(country_pvals$adf_pval), 3),
    pct_rejected_5 = round(mean(country_pvals$adf_pval < 0.05) * 100, 1)
  )
}

# Combine into a single summary table and display
urt_summary <- bind_rows(results)

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  PANEL UNIT ROOT TEST SUMMARY (Country-by-Country ADF)\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  H0: unit root (non-stationary) | Reject at 5% → stationary\n")
cat("  min_obs per country:", min_obs, "\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
print(urt_summary, row.names = FALSE)

cat("\n── Interpretation guide ──\n")
cat("  pct_rejected_5 > 70%  → broadly stationary, safe to use in levels\n")
cat("  pct_rejected_5 30-70% → mixed, inspect further or consider differencing\n")
cat("  pct_rejected_5 < 30%  → likely non-stationary, use in differences\n")

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERPRETATION OF RESULTS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# NOTE (unit root tests — revisit when writing paper):

# ADF results are uninformative here — expected with T≈20 (15–17 usable obs after lags).
# Low power means the test can't distinguish stationary from unit root.
# IPS would fix this but failed on unbalanced panel, so we're stuck with the power problem.

# Variables are safe in levels for the following reasons:
#   - inflation, GDP growth: growth rates, economically bounded
#   - reg_quality, corruption, pol_stability: bounded indices [-2.5, 2.5], unit root logically impossible
#   - credit_gdp: could be trending (financial deepening) — consider mentioning in paper
#   - MIMIC: high persistence (~3.8% rejection) is expected — informality is a slow-moving
#             structural characteristic, not a unit root problem; variation is mostly cross-country

# In paper: note T=20 limits formal IPS tests; justify stationarity via economic reasoning instead.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

```


```{r}
#| label: Diagnostics 2.0 Persistence Check on MIMIC (AR(1))
#| output: true


# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DIAGNOSTIC 2 — PERSISTENCE CHECK ON MIMIC (AR(1) Autocorrelation)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# Before using lagged MIMIC in the LP regression, we check how persistent
# it is. If MIMIC barely changes year-to-year (high AR(1)), then the lag
# is a reliable proxy for the current structural level of informality.

# WHAT WE COMPUTE:
#   Part A: Per-country AR(1) — correlation between MIMIC_t and MIMIC_{t-1}
#           within each country, then summarise across countries.
#   Part B: Pooled AR(1) regression — regress MIMIC_t on MIMIC_{t-1} across
#           the full panel.

# HOW TO INTERPRET:
#   AR(1) > 0.95 → extremely persistent; lagging is safe, but most variation
#                   is cross-country (confirmed by Diagnostic 3)
#   AR(1) 0.80–0.95 → highly persistent; lagging well-justified
#   AR(1) < 0.70 → moderate persistence; lagging may lose information

# IMPORTANT: We use dplyr::lag() explicitly throughout. Loading the tseries
# package can cause stats::lag to mask dplyr::lag — stats::lag does NOT
# shift values, it only changes a time-series attribute, producing incorrect
# results (a perfect AR(1) = 1 with zero residuals).
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ── Part A: Per-country AR(1) coefficient ──

ar1_country <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  arrange(country, year) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(mimic_t_minus1 = dplyr::lag(mimic, 1)) %>%
  filter(sum(!is.na(mimic) & !is.na(mimic_t_minus1)) >= 5) %>%
  summarise(
    n_pairs = sum(!is.na(mimic) & !is.na(mimic_t_minus1)),
    ar1 = cor(mimic, mimic_t_minus1, use = "complete.obs"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  MIMIC AR(1) — Per-Country Summary\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  Countries with enough data:", nrow(ar1_country), "\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
print(summary(ar1_country$ar1))

# ── Part B: Pooled AR(1) regression ──

mimic_ar <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  arrange(country, year) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(mimic_t_minus1 = dplyr::lag(mimic, 1)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(!is.na(mimic), !is.na(mimic_t_minus1))

cat("\n══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  MIMIC AR(1) — Pooled OLS Regression (mimic ~ mimic_t_minus1)\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  Coefficient on mimic_t_minus1 close to 1 → high persistence\n")
cat("  N observations:", nrow(mimic_ar), "\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
print(summary(lm(mimic ~ mimic_t_minus1, data = mimic_ar)))


# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERPRETATION OF RESULTS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Per-country AR(1): median = 0.91, mean = 0.87, 75th percentile = 0.95
# Pooled OLS: coefficient on mimic_t_minus1 = 0.994, R² = 0.998

# MIMIC is extremely persistent but NOT time-invariant. It moves slowly within
# countries (within-country SD ≈ 1 on a scale of 15–58, typical range ≈ 3.5
# points over 20 years). This is consistent with informality being a deep
# structural characteristic that evolves gradually.

# Implications for the LP specification:

#   1. LAGGING IS WELL-JUSTIFIED. With AR(1) ≈ 0.91–0.99, last year's MIMIC
#      is an excellent proxy for current informality. The lag also provides
#      the econometric benefit of pre-determination — it avoids the concern
#      that a monetary shock at time t could contemporaneously affect
#      informality at time t.

#   2. STANDALONE MIMIC CAN enter the LP equation alongside country FE,
#      because MIMIC is not perfectly collinear with the fixed effects.
#      However, country FE will absorb the vast majority of MIMIC's variation
#      (Diagnostic 3 will quantify the between vs. within share). The
#      standalone coefficient will be identified only off the small within-
#      country movements and may be imprecise. This is fine — the coefficient
#      of interest is δ_h on the interaction MPS × MIMIC, not γ_h on MIMIC
#      itself.

#   3. INTERACTION TERM MPS × MIMIC remains the core identification strategy.
#      It is well-identified because MPS varies substantially over time,
#      and the cross-country spread in MIMIC levels provides the moderating
#      variation. Even if country FE absorb most of the MIMIC level, the
#      interaction exploits the fact that the same MPS shock hits countries
#      with very different informality levels.

#   4. NAMESPACE WARNING: dplyr::lag() must be used explicitly throughout the
#      project. The tseries package causes stats::lag to mask dplyr::lag —
#      stats::lag does not shift values, producing silently wrong results.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

```


```{r}
#| label: Diagnostics 2.1 MIMIC Within-Country Variation Check

# Check: does MIMIC actually vary within any country?
mimic_check <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarise(
    n_obs = sum(!is.na(mimic)),
    sd_within = sd(mimic, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(n_obs >= 2)

cat("Countries with ANY within-country MIMIC variation:", 
    sum(mimic_check$sd_within > 0.001, na.rm = TRUE), 
    "out of", nrow(mimic_check), "\n")

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERPRETATION OF RESULTS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# All 158 countries with ≥2 observations show within-country MIMIC variation
# (sd > 0.001). MIMIC is NOT time-invariant — the earlier AR(1) = 1.000 result
# was caused by a namespace bug (stats::lag masking dplyr::lag), not by the data.
# After fixing with dplyr::lag(), AR(1) ≈ 0.91 (median), confirming genuine but
# slow-moving within-country variation.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════


```


```{r}
#| label: Diagnostics 2.2 MIMIC Within-Country Movement Magnitude

# How much does MIMIC actually move within countries?
mimic_variation <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  filter(sum(!is.na(mimic)) >= 5) %>%
  summarise(
    n_obs      = sum(!is.na(mimic)),
    mean_mimic = round(mean(mimic, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    sd_within  = round(sd(mimic, na.rm = TRUE), 6),   # 6 decimals to catch tiny variation
    min_mimic  = round(min(mimic, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    max_mimic  = round(max(mimic, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    range      = round(max(mimic, na.rm = TRUE) - min(mimic, na.rm = TRUE), 6),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("=== Within-country MIMIC variation summary ===\n\n")
print(summary(mimic_variation$sd_within))
cat("\n")
print(summary(mimic_variation$range))

# Show a few example countries to see the actual time series
cat("\n=== Sample countries: MIMIC over time ===\n")
examples <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  filter(country %in% c("Germany", "Brazil", "India", "Nigeria", "Hungary")) %>%
  select(country, year, mimic) %>%
  filter(!is.na(mimic)) %>%
  arrange(country, year)

print(examples)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERPRETATION OF RESULTS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# MIMIC genuinely varies but slowly: median within-country SD ≈ 1.1 (on a 15–58 scale),
# median range ≈ 3.5 over 20 years. Smooth, slowly-evolving series within countries
# (see Brazil, Hungary examples).

# Combined with Diagnostic 2.0 (median AR(1) = 0.91, pooled coefficient = 0.994):
#   - MIMIC is highly persistent but not constant
#   - Lagging is well-justified — MIMIC_{t-1} is an excellent proxy for current informality
#   - Most identification in MPS × MIMIC comes from cross-country variation in levels,
#     not within-country changes (Diagnostic 3 will quantify this)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

```


```{r}
#| label: Diagnostics 2.3 Lag Function Namespace Verification

# Check if the lag is actually shifting values
test <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  filter(country == "Brazil", !is.na(mimic)) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(
    lag_dplyr = dplyr::lag(mimic, 1),   # Explicitly call dplyr version
    lag_plain = lag(mimic, 1)            # Whatever is currently being called
  ) %>%
  select(country, year, mimic, lag_dplyr, lag_plain)

print(head(test, 10))

# BUGFIX: stats::lag() was silently overriding dplyr::lag() — lagged values were identical
# to contemporaneous values (no shift occurring). Use dplyr::lag() explicitly everywhere.
# This would have corrupted all lagged regressors in the LP regressions silently.

``` 


```{r}
#| label: Diagnostics 3. Within vs. Between Variation Decomposition
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DIAGNOSTIC 3 — WITHIN vs. BETWEEN VARIATION DECOMPOSITION
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# Panel data variation can be decomposed into two components:
#   - BETWEEN variation: differences across countries (e.g., Nigeria's MIMIC
#     is ~55, Germany's is ~15 — that gap is between variation)
#   - WITHIN variation: changes over time within a country (e.g., Brazil's
#     MIMIC moving from 39.8 to 36.2 over 2000–2013)

# WHY THIS MATTERS FOR THE LP SPECIFICATION:
# Country fixed effects absorb ALL between variation. So any regressor whose
# variation is mostly between-country will have its coefficient identified
# only from the small within-country movements. This is especially relevant
# for MIMIC, which we know is highly persistent (Diagnostic 2).

# For the interaction term MPS × MIMIC:
#   - MPS varies mostly within-country (shocks happen over time)
#   - MIMIC varies mostly between-country (structural characteristic)
#   - The interaction is well-identified because MPS provides time variation
#     and MIMIC provides cross-country moderation

# HOW TO INTERPRET:
#   Between_Pct close to 100% → variable is essentially a country characteristic;
#     country FE will absorb it, standalone coefficient poorly identified
#   Within_Pct close to 100% → variable moves mostly over time within countries;
#     well-identified even with country FE
#   Roughly balanced → both dimensions contribute to identification

# NOTE: Between_Pct + Within_Pct should sum to approximately 100%.
# Small deviations are due to the unbalanced panel (not all countries
# have the same number of observations).
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

vars_decomp <- c("mimic", "mps", "infl", "gdp_pc", "gdp_growth",
                 "credit_gdp", "reg_quality", "corruption", "pol_stability")

decomp <- lapply(vars_decomp, function(v) {
  # Step 1: Compute each country's time-series mean (the "between" component)
  means <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    group_by(country) %>%
    summarise(c_mean = mean(.data[[v]], na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

  # Step 2: Compute within-country deviations from each country's mean
  df_temp <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    left_join(means, by = "country") %>%
    mutate(within_dev = .data[[v]] - c_mean)

  # Step 3: Compute variance components
  total_var   <- var(df_world_2000_2019[[v]], na.rm = TRUE)
  between_var <- var(means$c_mean, na.rm = TRUE)
  within_var  <- var(df_temp$within_dev, na.rm = TRUE)

  data.frame(
    Variable    = v,
    Between_Pct = round(between_var / total_var * 100, 1),
    Within_Pct  = round(within_var  / total_var * 100, 1)
  )
})

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  WITHIN vs. BETWEEN VARIANCE DECOMPOSITION (%)\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  Between = cross-country differences (absorbed by country FE)\n")
cat("  Within  = over-time changes within a country (survives FE)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
print(do.call(rbind, decomp), row.names = FALSE)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERPRETATION OF RESULTS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Results match expectations and strongly support the identification strategy:

#   mimic:         99.6% between, 1.0% within — almost purely a country
#                  characteristic. Country FE will absorb the level; standalone
#                  MIMIC coefficient will be very poorly identified. Not a problem —
#                  the coefficient of interest is on the interaction, not MIMIC itself.

#   mps:           0.4% between, 99.6% within — pure time variation, as expected
#                  for a shock series centered on zero by construction.

#   infl:          29.3% between, 77.5% within — substantial within variation means
#                  the dependent variable moves enough over time to be explained.

#   gdp_pc:        91.5% between, 8.7% within — substantial cross country variation, 
#                  to be taken as a log dependent variable or log control.

#   gdp_growth:    16.5% between, 83.6% within — same as inflation; well-suited
#                  as a dependent variable or control.

#   credit_gdp:    88.5% between — mostly structural. Standalone coefficient weakly
#                  identified with country FE, but still useful as a control for
#                  cross-country financial depth differences.

#   reg_quality:   95.6% between — almost entirely cross-country.
#   corruption:    97.1% between — almost entirely cross-country.
#   pol_stability: 91.2% between — almost entirely cross-country.
#                  All three governance variables are 90%+ between. Including multiple
#                  in the baseline wastes degrees of freedom — pick one, relegate
#                  others to robustness (Diagnostic 4 correlation results will guide
#                  which one to keep).

# KEY TAKEAWAY FOR THE PAPER:
# The interaction MPS × MIMIC combines a 99.6%-within variable (MPS) with a
# 99.6%-between variable (MIMIC). The interaction inherits variation from both
# dimensions — it differs across countries (because MIMIC levels differ) AND
# over time (because MPS shocks arrive at different points). This makes the
# interaction well-identified even with country and year fixed effects.
# We state this explicitly in the methodology section.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

```


```{r}
#| label: Diagnostics 4. Multicollinearity Check
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DIAGNOSTIC 4 — MULTICOLLINEARITY CHECK (Correlation Matrix)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# If two regressors are very highly correlated (|r| > 0.85), including both
# in the same regression inflates standard errors and makes individual
# coefficients unreliable. We check all candidate control variables plus
# MIMIC to decide which controls to include in the baseline specification.

# WHY MIMIC IS INCLUDED:
# Informality is likely correlated with governance quality (countries with
# larger informal economies tend to have weaker institutions). If the
# correlation is very high, the interaction MPS × MIMIC could partly
# capture MPS × governance rather than MPS × informality. This doesn't
# invalidate the result, but it's important to understand and discuss.

# HOW TO INTERPRET:
#   |r| < 0.50  → no concern, include both freely
#   |r| 0.50–0.70 → moderate correlation, fine in practice
#   |r| 0.70–0.85 → notable, but usually acceptable; monitor SEs
#   |r| > 0.85  → high multicollinearity risk; avoid both in same spec,
#                  or pick one and use the other in robustness checks

# ACTION ITEMS (fill in after reviewing results):
#   - If reg_quality and corruption correlate > 0.85 → pick one for baseline
#   - If mimic correlates highly with a governance variable → note in paper
#     that the informality channel may partly proxy institutional quality
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

controls_cor <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(mimic, mps, credit_gdp, reg_quality, corruption, pol_stability,
         voice_acc, gdp_growth, gdp_pc, infl)

cor_matrix <- round(cor(controls_cor, use = "complete.obs"), 2)

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  CORRELATION MATRIX — MIMIC + Control Variables\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  Flag pairs with |r| > 0.85 for multicollinearity concern\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
print(cor_matrix)

# Automatically flag high correlations (excluding diagonal)
cat("\n── Pairs with |r| > 0.70 ──\n")
high_cor <- which(abs(cor_matrix) > 0.70 & upper.tri(cor_matrix), arr.ind = TRUE)
if (nrow(high_cor) > 0) {
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(high_cor))) {
    r <- high_cor[i, ]
    cat(sprintf("  %s  ↔  %s : r = %.2f\n",
                rownames(cor_matrix)[r[1]], colnames(cor_matrix)[r[2]],
                cor_matrix[r[1], r[2]]))
  }
} else {
  cat("  None found — all pairwise correlations below 0.70\n")
}

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERPRETATION OF RESULTS (WITH GDP_PC)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Five distinct patterns emerge:

# 1. GOVERNANCE BLOCK (reg_quality, corruption, pol_stability, voice_acc)
#    Still extremely tightly correlated (r = 0.71–0.93). The strongest pair is
#    reg_quality ↔ corruption at 0.93 — effectively redundant measures.
#    → ACTION: Include only ONE governance variable in the baseline.
#      Use others in robustness checks (one at a time).

# 2. DEVELOPMENT BLOCK (gdp_pc + governance + credit_gdp)
#    gdp_pc is strongly positively correlated with governance indicators
#    (r = 0.63–0.80) and credit_gdp (r = 0.63).
#    This indicates a broader “development” dimension: richer countries have
#    stronger institutions and deeper financial systems.
#    → IMPLICATION: Including gdp_pc together with governance variables risks
#      multicollinearity and conceptual overlap (institutions vs development level).
#    → ACTION: Avoid including gdp_pc and governance simultaneously in the
#      baseline; treat them as alternative controls.

# 3. MIMIC ↔ DEVELOPMENT / GOVERNANCE (r = -0.48 to -0.68)
#    MIMIC is strongly negatively correlated with both governance and gdp_pc
#    (e.g., gdp_pc: -0.66). Informality is clearly a “low development” feature.
#    → IMPLICATION: The MPS × MIMIC interaction may partly reflect broader
#      development effects, not just informality.
#    → ACTION: Control for either governance OR gdp_pc to isolate the channel.

# 4. CREDIT_GDP ↔ DEVELOPMENT (r = 0.63 with gdp_pc; 0.53–0.73 with governance)
#    credit_gdp loads on the same development dimension but remains below the
#    critical 0.85 threshold.
#    → ACTION: Safe to include with either gdp_pc or one governance variable,
#      but monitor standard errors.

# 5. MACRO VARIABLES (gdp_growth, infl)
#    Still weakly correlated with everything (|r| ≤ 0.30).
#    → ACTION: Clean controls; no multicollinearity concerns.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

```

```{r}
#| label: Clean Up — Diagnostic Temp Objects

# Remove all temporary objects created during Diagnostics 1–4.
# Only df_world_2000_2019 carries forward from here.
rm(
  vars_urt, min_obs, results, country_pvals, urt_summary,       # Diag 1
  ar1_country, mimic_ar,                                        # Diag 2.0
  mimic_check,                                                  # Diag 2.1
  mimic_variation, examples,                                    # Diag 2.2
  test,                                                         # Diag 2.3
  vars_decomp, decomp,                                          # Diag 3
  controls_cor, cor_matrix, high_cor,                           # Diag 4
  i, r, v # values
)

```

```{r}
#| label: Create Lagged Variables
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CREATE LAGGED VARIABLES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# All controls and the informality measure enter the LP regression as one-period
# lags (t-1). This ensures they are PRE-DETERMINED relative to the monetary
# policy shock at time t, avoiding simultaneity bias. For example, if MPS at
# time t pushes firms into informality, using MIMIC_{t-1} sidesteps that
# reverse causality because last year's informality can't be caused by this
# year's shock.

# Diagnostic 2 confirmed MIMIC is highly persistent (AR(1) ≈ 0.91), so
# MIMIC_{t-1} is an excellent proxy for the current structural level.

# CRITICAL: We use dplyr::lag() explicitly throughout — the tseries package
# causes stats::lag to mask dplyr::lag, which does NOT shift values
# (confirmed in Diagnostic 2.3).

# VARIABLES LAGGED:
#   - mimic (key moderating variable)
#   - gdp_growth, infl (macro controls)
#   - credit_gdp (financial depth control)
#   - pol_stability (baseline governance control)
#   - reg_quality, corruption, voice_acc (robustness governance alternatives)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  arrange(country, year) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(
    # --- Key moderating variable ---
    mimic_t_minus1         = dplyr::lag(mimic, 1),

    # --- Macro controls ---
    gdp_growth_t_minus1    = dplyr::lag(gdp_growth, 1),
    gdp_pc_t_minus1        = dplyr::lag(gdp_pc, 1),
    infl_t_minus1          = dplyr::lag(infl, 1),
    
    # Log GDP per capita
    lgdp_pc = log(gdp_pc),

    # --- Financial depth control ---
    credit_gdp_t_minus1    = dplyr::lag(credit_gdp, 1),

    # --- Governance: baseline ---
    pol_stability_t_minus1 = dplyr::lag(pol_stability, 1),

    # --- Governance: robustness alternatives ---
    reg_quality_t_minus1   = dplyr::lag(reg_quality, 1),
    corruption_t_minus1    = dplyr::lag(corruption, 1),
    voice_acc_t_minus1     = dplyr::lag(voice_acc, 1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Quick verification: show Brazil to confirm lags are correctly shifted
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  LAG VERIFICATION — Brazil (mimic and infl)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  filter(country == "Brazil") %>%
  select(country, year, mimic, mimic_t_minus1, infl, infl_t_minus1) %>%
  print(n = 10)

```


```{r}
#| label: Construct Forward-Looking Dependent Variables
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CONSTRUCT FORWARD-LOOKING DEPENDENT VARIABLES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# In local projections (Jordà 2005), we estimate a separate regression for
# each forecast horizon h. The dependent variable at horizon h is the
# CUMULATIVE CHANGE in the outcome from just before the shock (t-1) to
# the future period (t+h):

# Main dependent
#   d_infl_h      = π_{i,t+h} − π_{i,t−1}
#   log(d_gdp_pc_h) = d_lgdp_pc_h    = log(y_{i,t+h}) − log(y_{i,t−1})

#  Sideline dependent:
#   d_gdp_growth_h = g_{i,t+h} − g_{i,t−1}

# This measures the total impulse response: how much has inflation (or growth)
# moved h years after the monetary policy shock, relative to its pre-shock level.

# We construct these for horizons h = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
#   h = 0: contemporaneous response (same year as shock)
#   h = 1: one year after the shock
#   ...
#   h = 5: five years after the shock

# IMPORTANT: dplyr::lead() is used to pull future values. Like dplyr::lag(),
# this must be done WITHIN each country group to avoid pulling values from
# a different country. We also need lagged inflation/growth (t-1) as the
# baseline, which we already created in Phase 1.4.

# NOTE: Higher horizons (h = 4, 5) will have more missing values because
# for a country observed until 2019, h = 5 requires data from 2024 — which
# doesn't exist. The effective sample shrinks as h increases.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

max_horizon <- 5

df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  arrange(country, year) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(
    # Lagged values (baseline for cumulative change)
    infl_t_minus1       = dplyr::lag(infl, 1),
    gdp_pc_t_minus1      = dplyr::lag(gdp_pc, 1),
    lgdp_pc_t_minus1      = dplyr::lag(lgdp_pc, 1),
    gdp_growth_t_minus1 = dplyr::lag(gdp_growth, 1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Create cumulative change variables for each horizon h = 0 to max_horizon
for (h in 0:max_horizon) {
  # Inflation: d_infl_h = infl_{t+h} - infl_{t-1}
  infl_lead_name <- paste0("d_infl_h", h)
    # Log GDP per capita: d_lgdp_pc_h = lgdp_pc_{t+h} - lgdp_pc_{t-1}
  lgdp_pc_lead_name <- paste0("d_lgdp_pc_h", h)
  # GDP growth: d_gdp_growth_h = gdp_growth_{t+h} - gdp_growth_{t-1}
  gdp_growth_lead_name  <- paste0("d_gdp_growth_h", h)

  df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    arrange(country, year) %>%
    group_by(country) %>%
    mutate(
      !!infl_lead_name := dplyr::lead(infl, h) - infl_t_minus1,
      !!lgdp_pc_lead_name := dplyr::lead(lgdp_pc, h) - lgdp_pc_t_minus1,
      !!gdp_growth_lead_name  := dplyr::lead(gdp_growth, h) - gdp_growth_t_minus1
    ) %>%
    ungroup()
}

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Verification: show Brazil's inflation dependent variables
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  FORWARD-LOOKING MAIN DEPENDENT VARIABLES — Brazil (inflation)\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  d_infl_h0 = infl_t - infl_{t-1} (contemporaneous change)\n")
cat("  d_infl_h3 = infl_{t+3} - infl_{t-1} (cumulative change over 3 years)\n")
cat("  d_infl_h5 = infl_{t+5} - infl_{t-1} (cumulative change over 5 years)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  filter(country == "Brazil") %>%
  select(country, year, infl, infl_t_minus1, d_infl_h0, d_infl_h1, d_infl_h2, d_infl_h3, d_infl_h4, d_infl_h5) %>%
  print(n = 12)


# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Verification: show Brazil's GDP Growth dependent variables
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  FORWARD-LOOKING MAIN DEPENDENT VARIABLES — Brazil (Logged GDP per capita)\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  d_lgdp_pc_h0 = d_lgdp_pc_t - lgdp_pc_{t-1} (contemporaneous change)\n")
cat("  d_lgdp_pc_h3 = d_lgdp_pc_{t+3} - lgdp_pc_{t-1} (cumulative change over 3 years)\n")
cat("  d_lgdp_pc_h5 = d_lgdp_pc_{t+5} - lgdp_pc_{t-1} (cumulative change over 5 years)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  filter(country == "Brazil") %>%
  select(country, year, lgdp_pc, lgdp_pc_t_minus1, d_lgdp_pc_h0, d_lgdp_pc_h1, d_lgdp_pc_h2, d_lgdp_pc_h3, d_lgdp_pc_h4) %>%
  print(n = 12)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Verification: show Brazil's GDP Growth dependent variables
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  FORWARD-LOOKING SIDELINE DEPENDENT VARIABLES — Brazil (GDP Growth)\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  d_gdp_growth_h0 = gdp_growth_t - gdp_growth_{t-1} (contemporaneous change)\n")
cat("  d_gdp_growth_h3 = gdp_growth_{t+3} - gdp_growth_{t-1} (cumulative change over 3 years)\n")
cat("  d_gdp_growth_h5 = gdp_growth_{t+5} - gdp_growth_{t-1} (cumulative change over 5 years)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  filter(country == "Brazil") %>%
  select(country, year, gdp_growth, gdp_growth_t_minus1, d_gdp_growth_h0, d_gdp_growth_h1, d_gdp_growth_h2, d_gdp_growth_h3, d_gdp_growth_h4) %>%
  print(n = 12)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Show how sample size shrinks with horizon
cat("\n── Non-missing observations by horizon ──\n")
horizon_coverage <- data.frame(
  horizon = 0:max_horizon,
  n_infl  = sapply(0:max_horizon, function(h) sum(!is.na(df_world_2000_2019[[paste0("d_infl_h", h)]]))),
  n_gdp_pc  = sapply(0:max_horizon, function(h) sum(!is.na(df_world_2000_2019[[paste0("d_lgdp_pc_h", h)]]))),
  n_gdp_growth   = sapply(0:max_horizon, function(h) sum(!is.na(df_world_2000_2019[[paste0("d_gdp_growth_h", h)]])))
)
print(horizon_coverage, row.names = FALSE)


```


```{r}
#| label: Create Interaction Terms
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CREATE INTERACTION TERMS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# The core identification strategy relies on the interaction MPS × MIMIC.
# The coefficient δ_h on this interaction captures how the effect of a
# monetary policy shock on inflation DEPENDS on the level of informality.

#   δ_h > 0 → higher informality DAMPENS the inflation response to MPS
#   δ_h < 0 → higher informality AMPLIFIES the inflation response
#   δ_h ≈ 0 → informality doesn't moderate monetary transmission

# We use LAGGED MIMIC (t-1) in the interaction to maintain pre-determination.
# Since MIMIC is 99.6% between-country (Diagnostic 3), the interaction
# effectively asks: "does the same MPS shock produce a different response
# in high-informality vs. low-informality countries?"
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  mutate(
    # --- Core interaction ---
    mps_x_mimic = mps * mimic_t_minus1
  )

# Quick summary of the interaction term
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  INTERACTION TERM SUMMARY: mps_x_mimic = MPS × MIMIC_{t-1}\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
cat("Non-missing observations:", sum(!is.na(df_world_2000_2019$mps_x_mimic)), "\n\n")
print(summary(df_world_2000_2019$mps_x_mimic))

```

```{r}
#| label: Visualize Outliers 1. — Shock and Macro Variables
#| output: true


# Group 1: MPS, Inflation, GDP Growth (the variables most likely to need winsorization)
vars_group1 <- c("mps", "infl", "gdp_growth")

df_box1 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(dev_status, all_of(vars_group1)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(dev_status)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(vars_group1), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  filter(!is.na(value))

ggplot(df_box1, aes(x = dev_status, y = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "red", outlier.alpha = 0.4, outlier.size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Shock & Macro Variables: Boxplots by Development Status",
       subtitle = "Red dots = outliers beyond 1.5×IQR | Before winsorization",
       x = NULL, y = "Value") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplot(df_box1, aes(x = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Shock & Macro Variables: Histograms",
       x = "Value", y = "Count", fill = "Status") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif")

```

```{r}
#| label: Visualize Outliers 2. — Informality and Financial Depth
#| output: true
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4

# Group 2: MIMIC, Credit/GDP, Political Stability (informality + baseline controls)
vars_group2 <- c("mimic", "credit_gdp", "pol_stability")

df_box2 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(dev_status, all_of(vars_group2)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(dev_status)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(vars_group2), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  filter(!is.na(value))

ggplot(df_box2, aes(x = dev_status, y = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "red", outlier.alpha = 0.4, outlier.size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Informality & Baseline Controls — Boxplots by Development Status",
       subtitle = "Red dots = outliers beyond 1.5×IQR | Before winsorization",
       x = NULL, y = "Value") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplot(df_box2, aes(x = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Informality & Baseline Controls — Histograms",
       x = "Value", y = "Count", fill = "Status") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif")

```


```{r}
#| label: Visualize Outliers 3. — Governance Variables
#| output: true
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4

# Group 3: Regulatory Quality, Corruption, Voice & Accountability (robustness governance)
vars_group3 <- c("reg_quality", "corruption", "voice_acc")

df_box3 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(dev_status, all_of(vars_group3)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(dev_status)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(vars_group3), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  filter(!is.na(value))

ggplot(df_box3, aes(x = dev_status, y = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "red", outlier.alpha = 0.4, outlier.size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Governance Variables — Boxplots by Development Status",
       subtitle = "Red dots = outliers beyond 1.5×IQR | Before winsorization",
       x = NULL, y = "Value") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplot(df_box3, aes(x = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Governance Variables — Histograms",
       x = "Value", y = "Count", fill = "Status") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif")

```


```{r}
#| label: Visualize Outliers 4. All 9 boxplots & histogr combined
#| output: true


# Visualize the distribution of ALL specification variables
# to identify outliers before deciding on winsorization

vars_outlier <- c("mps", "infl", "gdp_growth", "mimic", "credit_gdp",
                  "pol_stability", "reg_quality", "corruption", "voice_acc")

# --- Boxplots by development status ---
df_box <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(dev_status, all_of(vars_outlier)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(dev_status)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(vars_outlier), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  filter(!is.na(value))

ggplot(df_box, aes(x = dev_status, y = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "red", outlier.alpha = 0.4, outlier.size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of All Specification Variables by Development Status",
       subtitle = "Red dots = outliers beyond 1.5×IQR | Before any winsorization",
       x = NULL, y = "Value") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# --- Histograms to see the full shape of each distribution ---
ggplot(df_box, aes(x = value, fill = dev_status)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Histograms of All Specification Variables",
       subtitle = "Look for heavy tails and extreme outliers",
       x = "Value", y = "Count", fill = "Status") +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "serif")


```

```{r}
#| label: Visualize Outliers 4. - Extreme Values Table
#| output: true

# Print the most extreme values for all specification variables
vars_outlier <- c("mps", "infl", "gdp_growth", "mimic", "credit_gdp",
                  "pol_stability", "reg_quality", "corruption", "voice_acc")

cat("── Top 5 most extreme values (both tails) ──\n")
for (v in vars_outlier) {
  cat(sprintf("\n%s — LOWEST 5:\n", toupper(v)))
  df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    filter(!is.na(.data[[v]])) %>%
    arrange(.data[[v]]) %>%
    select(country, year, dev_status, all_of(v)) %>%
    head(5) %>%
    print()

  cat(sprintf("\n%s — HIGHEST 5:\n", toupper(v)))
  df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    filter(!is.na(.data[[v]])) %>%
    arrange(desc(.data[[v]])) %>%
    select(country, year, dev_status, all_of(v)) %>%
    head(5) %>%
    print()
}

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INTERPRETATION OF OUTLIER ANALYSIS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# 1. WINSORIZE — MPS, INFLATION, GDP GROWTH
#    These three variables show severe outliers that would dominate OLS:

#    MPS: Turkey 2000 = 68.6, roughly 36× the emerging-market SD. Turkey
#    alone accounts for 4 of the 5 lowest and 2 of the 5 highest MPS values.
#    A single country should not drive the entire regression.

#    Inflation: Hyperinflation episodes — DRC at 514, Angola at 325.
#    Genuine but orders of magnitude above the median (~4.5).

#    GDP growth: Conflict-driven swings — Libya goes from -50.3 (2011) to
#    +86.8 (2012) in consecutive years. Extreme but mechanically influential.

#    → ACTION: Winsorize all three at 1st/99th percentiles in Phase 1.7.

# 2. DO NOT WINSORIZE — GDP per Capita
#    Variable is already logged, no need to winsorize it

# 3. DO NOT WINSORIZE — MIMIC
#    Range is 8.1 (USA) to 68.2 (Bolivia). These are NOT outliers — they are
#    the actual cross-country spread that identifies the MPS × MIMIC
#    interaction. Capping the extremes would compress exactly the variation
#    the paper needs.

# 4. DO NOT WINSORIZE — CREDIT/GDP
#    Iceland at 305 (pre-crisis banking boom) looks extreme but enters as
#    a lagged control, not a dependent variable. The value is economically
#    real and its influence on the regression is limited.

# 5. DO NOT WINSORIZE — GOVERNANCE VARIABLES
#    All bounded on a fixed [-2.5, 2.5] scale. Extremes (Somalia at -3.05
#    for pol_stability) are near-boundary values, not data errors.
#    Winsorizing bounded indices is not meaningful.

# 6. ROBUSTNESS FLAG — TURKEY
#    Turkey is extraordinarily influential in MPS (max = 68.6, min = -19.5).
#    Even after winsorizing, it will pull weight. Add a robustness check
#    excluding Turkey entirely to show results are not driven by one country.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

```
```{r}
#| label: Clean Up — Outlier Visualization Temp Objects

# Remove temporary plot data frames and helper vectors from outlier visualization.
# df_world_2000_2019 carries forward for winsorization.
rm(
  vars_group1, vars_group2, vars_group3, vars_outlier,
  df_box1, df_box2, df_box3, df_box,
  gdp_growth_lead_name, gdp_lead_name, gdp_pc_lead_name, h, infl_lead_name,
  lgdp_pc_lead_name, v
)

```

```{r}
#| label: Outlier Treatment (Winsorization)
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# OUTLIER TREATMENT (WINSORIZATION)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# Extreme values in MPS, inflation, and GDP growth can dominate OLS estimates.
# From the descriptive statistics:
#   - MPS in emerging markets: max = 68.65 (vs. SD ≈ 1.91)
#   - Inflation: max = 513.9 (vs. median ≈ 4.5)
#   - GDP growth: min = -50.34, max = 86.83

# These are genuine observations (hyperinflation episodes, extreme policy
# moves) but a few such values can disproportionately drive regression
# coefficients in a sample of thousands.

# WINSORIZATION replaces values beyond the 1st and 99th percentiles with
# the value at that percentile. This retains the observation (unlike
# trimming/dropping) but caps its influence.

# We create BOTH winsorized and original versions so results can be
# shown both ways (main specification uses winsorized; robustness uses raw).

# NOTE: Winsorization is applied to the RAW variables. The forward-looking
# dependent variables (d_infl_h*, d_gdp_growth_h*) were constructed from the raw
# values, so we rebuild them after winsorization. Alternatively, we could
# winsorize the dependent variables directly — we do both and keep the
# directly winsorized versions for simplicity.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Helper function: winsorize a vector at given lower and upper quantiles
winsorize <- function(x, probs = c(0.01, 0.99)) {
  q <- quantile(x, probs = probs, na.rm = TRUE)
  x[x < q[1] & !is.na(x)] <- q[1]
  x[x > q[2] & !is.na(x)] <- q[2]
  return(x)
}

# --- Show pre-winsorization extremes ---
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  OUTLIER TREATMENT — Before vs. After Winsorization (1st/99th)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")

vars_to_winsorize <- c("mps", "infl", "gdp_growth")

cat("── BEFORE winsorization ──\n")
for (v in vars_to_winsorize) {
  q <- quantile(df_world_2000_2019[[v]], probs = c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)
  cat(sprintf("  %s: min = %.2f, max = %.2f | p1 = %.2f, p99 = %.2f\n",
              v,
              min(df_world_2000_2019[[v]], na.rm = TRUE),
              max(df_world_2000_2019[[v]], na.rm = TRUE),
              q[1], q[2]))
}

# --- Apply winsorization ---
df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  mutate(
    mps_w        = winsorize(mps),
    infl_w       = winsorize(infl),
    gdp_growth_w = winsorize(gdp_growth)
  )

cat("\n── AFTER winsorization (new _w variables) ──\n")
for (v in paste0(vars_to_winsorize, "_w")) {
  cat(sprintf("  %s: min = %.2f, max = %.2f\n",
              v,
              min(df_world_2000_2019[[v]], na.rm = TRUE),
              max(df_world_2000_2019[[v]], na.rm = TRUE)))
}

# --- Rebuild winsorized dependent variables and interaction ---
# Winsorized lagged values
df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  arrange(country, year) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  mutate(
    infl_w_t_minus1       = dplyr::lag(infl_w, 1),
    gdp_growth_w_t_minus1 = dplyr::lag(gdp_growth_w, 1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Winsorized forward-looking dependent variables
for (h in 0:max_horizon) {
  infl_lead_name <- paste0("d_infl_w_h", h)
  gdp_growth_lead_name  <- paste0("d_gdp_growth_w_h", h)

  df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    arrange(country, year) %>%
    group_by(country) %>%
    mutate(
      !!infl_lead_name := dplyr::lead(infl_w, h) - infl_w_t_minus1,
      !!gdp_growth_lead_name  := dplyr::lead(gdp_growth_w, h) - gdp_growth_w_t_minus1
    ) %>%
    ungroup()
}

# Winsorized interaction
df_world_2000_2019 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  mutate(mps_w_x_mimic = mps_w * mimic_t_minus1)

cat("\n── Observations affected by winsorization ──\n")
cat(sprintf("  mps:        %d values capped\n",
            sum(df_world_2000_2019$mps != df_world_2000_2019$mps_w, na.rm = TRUE)))
cat(sprintf("  infl:       %d values capped\n",
            sum(df_world_2000_2019$infl != df_world_2000_2019$infl_w, na.rm = TRUE)))
cat(sprintf("  gdp_growth: %d values capped\n",
            sum(df_world_2000_2019$gdp_growth != df_world_2000_2019$gdp_growth_w, na.rm = TRUE)))

```


```{r}
#| label: Document Effective Estimation Sample
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DOCUMENT THE EFFECTIVE ESTIMATION SAMPLE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# The effective estimation sample is the subset of country-year observations
# where ALL variables needed for the baseline LP regression are non-missing
# simultaneously. This is typically much smaller than the raw panel because
# missingness in different variables drops different observations.

# The baseline LP regression at horizon h requires:
#   Dependent:   d_infl_w_h{h}, d_lgdp_pc_h{h}, or d_gdp_growth_w_h{h}
#   Regressors:  mps_w, mimic_t_minus1, mps_w_x_mimic,
#                gdp_growth_w_t_minus1, infl_w_t_minus1, credit_gdp_t_minus1,
#                pol_stability_t_minus1
#   Fixed effects: country, year

# We report the sample at h = 0 (largest possible) and show how it shrinks
# with each horizon increase. This table goes in the paper's data section.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Define baseline regressors (same for all horizons)
baseline_regressors <- c("mps_w", "mimic_t_minus1", "mps_w_x_mimic",
                         "gdp_growth_w_t_minus1", "infl_w_t_minus1",
                         "credit_gdp_t_minus1", "pol_stability_t_minus1",
                         "country", "year")

# For each horizon, count complete cases
sample_summary <- data.frame(
  horizon       = 0:max_horizon,
  n_obs_infl    = NA_integer_,
  n_obs_lgdp    = NA_integer_,
  n_countries_infl = NA_integer_,
  n_countries_lgdp = NA_integer_
)

for (h in 0:max_horizon) {
  # Inflation sample
  infl_dep <- paste0("d_infl_w_h", h)
  infl_vars <- c(infl_dep, baseline_regressors)
  infl_complete <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    select(all_of(infl_vars)) %>%
    na.omit()
  sample_summary$n_obs_infl[h + 1]       <- nrow(infl_complete)
  sample_summary$n_countries_infl[h + 1]  <- n_distinct(infl_complete$country)

  # Log GDP per capita sample
  lgdp_dep <- paste0("d_lgdp_pc_h", h)
  lgdp_vars <- c(lgdp_dep, baseline_regressors)
  lgdp_complete <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
    select(all_of(lgdp_vars)) %>%
    na.omit()
  sample_summary$n_obs_lgdp[h + 1]       <- nrow(lgdp_complete)
  sample_summary$n_countries_lgdp[h + 1]  <- n_distinct(lgdp_complete$country)
}

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  EFFECTIVE ESTIMATION SAMPLE BY HORIZON\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  All baseline regressors + dependent variable non-missing\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
print(sample_summary, row.names = FALSE)

# Detailed breakdown at h = 0: average time-series length per country
h0_infl_vars <- c("d_infl_w_h0", baseline_regressors)
h0_sample <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(all_of(h0_infl_vars)) %>%
  na.omit()

ts_length <- h0_sample %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarise(T_i = n(), .groups = "drop")

cat("\n── Baseline sample details (h = 0, inflation) ──\n")
cat("  Total observations:", nrow(h0_sample), "\n")
cat("  Countries:         ", n_distinct(h0_sample$country), "\n")
cat("  Avg years/country: ", round(mean(ts_length$T_i), 1), "\n")
cat("  Min years/country: ", min(ts_length$T_i), "\n")
cat("  Max years/country: ", max(ts_length$T_i), "\n")

```


```{r}
#| label: Verify Panel Structure and Apply Minimum Threshold
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# VERIFY PANEL STRUCTURE & APPLY MINIMUM THRESHOLD
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# Countries with very few usable observations (e.g., 1–3 years in the
# estimation sample) contribute almost no identifying variation and can
# introduce noise. LP estimation needs enough time-series depth per country
# to capture dynamics — a minimum of 5 years is a common threshold.
#
# We check the distribution of per-country observation counts in the
# effective sample and flag countries below the threshold for exclusion.
# This is done on the h = 0 sample (most complete horizon).

# WHAT THIS CHUNK DOES:
#   1. Show distribution of per-country time-series lengths
#   2. List countries below the threshold
#   3. Create a filtered "estimation ready" dataset excluding thin countries
#   4. Report the final sample size
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

min_T <- 5  # Minimum usable years per country

# Step 1: Per-country observation count in the effective sample (h = 0)
h0_vars <- c("d_infl_w_h0", baseline_regressors)

country_T <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  select(all_of(h0_vars)) %>%
  na.omit() %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarise(T_i = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(T_i)

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  PER-COUNTRY TIME-SERIES LENGTH IN ESTIMATION SAMPLE (h = 0)\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
print(summary(country_T$T_i))

# Step 2: Flag countries below threshold
thin_countries <- country_T %>% filter(T_i < min_T)

cat(sprintf("\n── Countries with fewer than %d usable years: %d ──\n",
            min_T, nrow(thin_countries)))
if (nrow(thin_countries) > 0) {
  print(thin_countries, n = nrow(thin_countries))
} else {
  cat("  None — all countries meet the minimum threshold\n")
}

# Step 3: Identify countries that pass the threshold
keep_countries <- country_T %>% filter(T_i >= min_T) %>% pull(country)

# Create the estimation-ready dataset
# This is the dataset that will be used for ALL LP regressions going forward
df_est <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  filter(country %in% keep_countries)

# Step 4: Report final sample
cat(sprintf("\n══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n"))
cat(sprintf("  FINAL ESTIMATION-READY DATASET: df_est\n"))
cat(sprintf("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n"))
cat(sprintf("  Countries retained:   %d (of %d in full panel)\n",
            n_distinct(df_est$country), n_distinct(df_world_2000_2019$country)))
cat(sprintf("  Countries dropped:    %d (fewer than %d usable years)\n",
            nrow(thin_countries), min_T))
cat(sprintf("  Total rows in df_est: %d\n", nrow(df_est)))
cat(sprintf("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n"))

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PHASE 1 COMPLETE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# The dataset df_est is now estimation-ready for Phase 2 (descriptive
# visualizations) and Phase 3 (LP regressions).

# Contents of df_est:
#   Identifiers:  country, year, code, continent_code, dev_status, dev_binary
#   Shock:        mps, mps_w (winsorized)
#   Informality:  mimic, mimic_t_minus1
#   Interaction:  mps_x_mimic, mps_w_x_mimic (winsorized)
#   Dep vars:     d_infl_h0–h5, d_gdp_growth_h0–h5 (raw)
#                 d_infl_w_h0–h5 (winsorized), d_lgdp_pc_h0–h5 (raw log)
#                 d_gdp_growth_w_h0–h5 (winsorized, sideline)
#   Controls:     gdp_growth_t_minus1, infl_t_minus1, credit_gdp_t_minus1,
#                 pol_stability_t_minus1 (baseline)
#                 reg_quality_t_minus1, corruption_t_minus1, voice_acc_t_minus1 (robustness)
#   Winsorized:   gdp_growth_w_t_minus1, infl_w_t_minus1
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

```


```{r}
#| label: Clean Up — Estimation Sample Temp Objects

# Remove temporary objects from sample documentation and panel verification.
# Keep: df_world_2000_2019, df_est, keep_countries, baseline_regressors
rm(
  sample_summary, infl_complete, gdp_complete,
  h0_infl_vars, h0_sample, ts_length,
  h0_vars, country_T, thin_countries, min_T
)

```

```{r}
#| label: Geographical Coverage — Full Panel vs Estimation Sample
#| output: true
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GEOGRAPHICAL COVERAGE MAPS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Two maps side by side:
#   Map 1: All countries in the full panel (df_world_2000_2019)
#   Map 2: Countries retained in the estimation-ready sample (df_est)
# This visualizes which countries were lost during Phase 1 filtering
# (missingness, minimum observation threshold, etc.)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# --- Load world polygons ---
world <- rnaturalearth::ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") %>%
  mutate(iso3c = iso_a3)

# --- Map 1: Full panel ---
full_iso3 <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  distinct(country) %>%
  mutate(iso3c = countrycode(country, "country.name", "iso3c")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(iso3c)) %>%
  pull(iso3c)

world_full <- world %>%
  mutate(in_sample = if_else(iso3c %in% full_iso3, "In sample", "Not in sample"))

n_full <- sum(world_full$in_sample == "In sample", na.rm = TRUE)

p1 <- ggplot(world_full) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = in_sample), linewidth = 0.1, color = "grey30") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("In sample" = "#2C7FB8", "Not in sample" = "grey85")) +
  labs(title = paste0("Full Panel (", n_full, " Countries)"),
       subtitle = "All countries in df_world_2000_2019 before Phase 1 filtering",
       fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11, base_family = "serif") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

print(p1)

```

```{r}
#| label: Geographical Coverage — Estimation Sample by Development Status
#| output: true
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# --- Map 2: Estimation sample, colored by development status ---
est_countries <- df_est %>%
  distinct(country, dev_status) %>%
  mutate(iso3c = countrycode(country, "country.name", "iso3c")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(iso3c))

world_est <- world %>%
  left_join(est_countries %>% select(iso3c, dev_status), by = "iso3c") %>%
  mutate(
    map_status = case_when(
      dev_status == "Developed" ~ "Developed",
      dev_status == "Emerging"  ~ "Emerging",
      TRUE                      ~ "Not in sample"
    )
  )

# Ensure draw order: Not in sample behind, Emerging and Developed on top
world_est$map_status <- factor(world_est$map_status,
                               levels = c("Not in sample", "Developed", "Emerging"))

n_est       <- sum(!is.na(est_countries$country))
n_developed <- sum(est_countries$dev_status == "Developed", na.rm = TRUE)
n_emerging  <- sum(est_countries$dev_status == "Emerging", na.rm = TRUE)

p2 <- ggplot(world_est) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = map_status), linewidth = 0.1, color = "grey30") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Developed"     = "#2C7FB8",
                               "Emerging"       = "#D95F02",
                               "Not in sample"  = "grey85")) +
  labs(title = paste0("Estimation Sample (", n_est, " Countries: ",
                      n_developed, " Developed, ", n_emerging, " Emerging)"),
       subtitle = "Countries retained in df_est after Phase 1 filtering",
       fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11, base_family = "serif") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

print(p2)

# --- Summary of countries lost ---
lost_countries <- df_world_2000_2019 %>%
  distinct(country) %>%
  filter(!country %in% keep_countries) %>%
  mutate(iso3c = countrycode(country, "country.name", "iso3c"))

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  GEOGRAPHICAL COVERAGE SUMMARY\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat(sprintf("  Full panel:        %d countries\n", n_distinct(df_world_2000_2019$country)))
cat(sprintf("  Estimation sample: %d countries (%d Developed, %d Emerging)\n",
            n_est, n_developed, n_emerging))
cat(sprintf("  Countries lost:    %d\n", nrow(lost_countries)))
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")

if (nrow(lost_countries) > 0) {
  cat("\n── Countries dropped during Phase 1 ──\n")
  print(lost_countries %>% arrange(country), n = nrow(lost_countries))
}

# BIG NOTE!
# IN THE SUBSEQUENT SECTIONS: Turkey and Vietnam are included while in the results and of the paper they are not.

```


```{r}
#| label: Clean Up — Map and Phase 1 Temp Objects

# Remove all map-related temp objects.
# Going into Phase 2, the environment should contain only:
#   df_world_2000_2019  (full working panel with all variables)
#   df_est              (estimation-ready subset)
#   keep_countries      (vector of retained country names)
#   baseline_regressors (vector of regressor names for LP)
#   max_horizon         (integer, = 5)
rm(
  world, full_iso3, world_full, n_full, p1,
  est_countries, world_est, n_est, n_developed, n_emerging, p2,
  lost_countries
)

```

```{r}

# From easiest to hardest
# 1. PCA as main spec, and other could be used a robustness check
# 2. Causal connection (check email)
# 3. Connecting clusters of countries that are similar structure but differ on informality
# 4. IV - contact “ROUTLEDGE HANDBOOK OF THE INFORMAL ECONOMY”


```


```{r}
#| label: Governance PCA Composite
#| output: true

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GOVERNANCE COMPOSITE — PRINCIPAL COMPONENT ANALYSIS (PCA)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# PURPOSE:
# Diagnostic 4 showed that the four governance variables are highly inter-
# correlated (r = 0.71–0.90). Including any single one in the baseline is
# a defensible but somewhat arbitrary choice. PCA extracts a single composite
# score that captures the common variation across all four, sidestepping the
# "which one?" question.

# WHAT WE DO:
#   1. Run PCA on the four LAGGED governance variables (t-1) — for use in
#      the main LP specification where all controls are pre-determined.
#   2. Run PCA on the four CONTEMPORANEOUS governance variables — for use
#      in robustness checks or specifications without the pre-determination
#      requirement.
#   3. Report variance explained and loadings for both.
#   4. Attach PC1 scores as gov_pca_t_minus1 and gov_pca to the dataframe.

# HOW TO INTERPRET:
#   High gov_pca → strong institutions (high reg_quality,
#   low corruption, stable politics, strong voice/accountability)
#   Low gov_pca  → weak institutions

# NOTE: PCA scores are standardized (mean = 0, sd = 1) by construction.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ── Part A: LAGGED governance PCA (t-1) — for main LP specification ──

gov_vars_lag <- c("reg_quality_t_minus1", "corruption_t_minus1",
                  "pol_stability_t_minus1", "voice_acc_t_minus1")

gov_lag_idx <- complete.cases(df_world_2000_2019[, gov_vars_lag])

pca_lag <- prcomp(df_world_2000_2019[gov_lag_idx, gov_vars_lag], scale. = TRUE)

cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
cat("  GOVERNANCE PCA — LAGGED (t-1)\n")
cat("──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
cat("  Variables:", paste(gov_vars_lag, collapse = ", "), "\n")
cat("  Complete cases:", sum(gov_lag_idx), "of", nrow(df_world_2000_2019), "\n")
cat("══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")

cat("── Variance explained ──\n")
print(round(summary(pca_lag)$importance, 3))

cat("\n── PC1 loadings ──\n")
print(round(pca_lag$rotation[, 1], 3))

# Attach lagged PC1 scores
df_world_2000_2019$gov_pca_t_minus1 <- NA_real_
df_world_2000_2019$gov_pca_t_minus1[gov_lag_idx] <- pca_lag$x[, 1]

cat(sprintf("\n── gov_pca_t_minus1 created: %d non-missing values ──\n",
            sum(!is.na(df_world_2000_2019$gov_pca_t_minus1))))
print(summary(df_world_2000_2019$gov_pca_t_minus1))
```
